title: Section 1
summary: Deals with the path of Shiva (divine consciousness), realizing the universe as one's own body, and expansion of awareness. Addresses seeking universal oneness and spiritual identity.

sections.summary: Themes: self-knowledge, mind and meditation, freedom from attachment. The passage moves from “Consciousness which is omniscient and omnipotent (caitanyam) (is) the Self or true nature of Reality (ātmā)” through “He is a master (īśaḥ) of (his) senses (vīra)1 who is an enjoyer (of the aforesaid "ābhoga"2 or divine delight) (bhoktā) in the triad (of waking,…” through “(The other supernormal powers of the enlightened Yogī are: the power of) putting together (sandhāna) existent entities (bhūta); (the power of)…”

The passage moves from “Full acquisition (siddhiḥ) of mastery (īśatva) over the collective group of powers (cakra) (is achieved by the Yogī) through the emergence…” through “By uniting (anusandhānāt) with the Great (mahā) Lake (hrada), (the Yogī has) the experience (anubhavaḥ) of the generative source --virility or…”

verses.translation: Consciousness which is omniscient and omnipotent (caitanyam) (is) the Self or true nature of Reality (ātmā)
The (limited or contracted) knowledge (jñānam) (is) bondage (bandhaḥ)
The source (yoni) (and her) progeny (vargaḥ), (along with) that whose form (śarīram) is activity (kalā) (are also bondage)
The basis (adhiṣṭhānam) of the (limited and contracted) knowledge (jñāna) (is) the un-understood Mother (mātṛkā)
Bhairava --Supreme Being-- (bhairavaḥ) (is) a sudden flash or elevation of divine Consciousness (udyamaḥ)
Through union (sandhāne) with the collective group (cakra) of powers (śakti), (there is) the disappearance (saṁhāraḥ) of the universe (viśva)

(Even) during such different (states of consciousness) (bhede) as waking (jāgrat), dreaming (svapna) and profound sleep (suṣupta), there is (sambhavaḥ) the delight and enjoyment (ābhoga) of the Fourth State (turya)
Knowledge (jñānam) (is) the waking state of consciousness (jāgrat)
The dream state of consciousness (svapnaḥ) (is) thoughts and ideations (vikalpāḥ)
Non-discernment or lack of awareness (avivekaḥ) (is) the profound sleep (sauṣuptam) of Māyā --delusion-- (māyā)
He is a master (īśaḥ) of (his) senses (vīra)1 who is an enjoyer (of the aforesaid "ābhoga"2 or divine delight) (bhoktā) in the triad (of waking, dreaming and deep sleep) (tritaya)
The stages (bhūmikāḥ) of Yoga (yoga) (are) a fascinating wonder (vismayaḥ)
The Will (icchā) power (śaktiḥ) (of the enlightened Yogī is) the "Splendor of Śiva" (umā) (that is) Kumārī (kumārī)
All phenomena (outer or inner) (dṛśyam) (are) the body (śarīram) (of the enlightened Yogī)

Through the union (saṅghaṭṭāt) of the mind (citta) on the core of Consciousness (hṛdaye), there is appearance (darśanam) of (all) phenomena (outer or inner) (dṛśya) (and even) the void (svāpa) (as they are in their essential reality)

Or (vā) by union (sandhānāt) with the Pure (śuddha) Principle (tattva), (the Yogī becomes like one in whom) the (binding) power (śaktiḥ) (existing in) a limited and conditioned being (paśu) is absent (a)
Firm and unwavering awareness (vitarkaḥ) (that I am Śiva is) knowledge (jñānam) of the Self (ātma)
The Bliss (that the Yogī feels in abiding as the Knower or Perceiver) (ānandaḥ) of the world (—consisting of subjects and objects—) (loka) (is his) delight (sukham) of Samādhi (samādhi)
On being united (sandhāne) with the Power (of Will) (śakti) (there is) production or creation (utpattiḥ) of bodies (śarīra) (according to the Yogī's desire)

(The other supernormal powers of the enlightened Yogī are: the power of) putting together (sandhāna) existent entities (bhūta); (the power of) separating (pṛthaktva) them all --i.e. the existent entities-- (bhūta) (and the power of) assembling --saṅghaṭṭa-- (saṅghaṭṭāḥ) everything (viśva) (what has been separated by space and time)
Full acquisition (siddhiḥ) of mastery (īśatva) over the collective group of powers (cakra) (is achieved by the Yogī) through the emergence (udayāt) of Śuddhavidyā (śuddhavidyā)
By uniting (anusandhānāt) with the Great (mahā) Lake (hrada), (the Yogī has) the experience (anubhavaḥ) of the generative source --virility or potency-- (vīrya) of (all) mantra-s (mantra)
